Parallel phenotypic screens for acidification, siderophore production (CAS assay), and cellulase activity will link mechanisms to phylogeny. Data will be analyzed using multivariate statistics (e.g., PERMANOVA) to correlate phylogenetic distance with functional trait matrices.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a predictive functional map linking airborne microbial phylogeny to substrate solubilization ecology. It will identify keystone phylogenetic groups for specific nutrient mobilization processes and provide a cultured model system for testing hypotheses on evolutionary drivers of microbial weathering strategies in atmospheric deposition contexts.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior strain-by-strain catalogs, this study employs a deliberate comparative phylogeny-function framework to uncover conserved strategies, moving from descriptive listing to predictive ecological mapping for airborne microbes.

proposals.title: In situ quantification of fungal-mediated zinc solubilization in contaminated soil using microdialysis sampling
proposals.motivation: Zinc contamination of soils is a persistent environmental issue, and microbially-mediated solubilization is a key process for both bioremediation and phytoextraction. However, current methods for measuring metal mobilization, such as batch extraction or destructive soil sampling, fail to capture the dynamic, spatially-resolved activity of fungi within the intact, heterogeneous soil matrix. This limits our understanding of real-time microbial function in complex environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in vivo microdialysis, adapted from neuroscience, can provide real-time, in situ measurements of zinc solubilization by metal-transforming fungi in soil, and that this activity will correlate with fungal community diversity and metabolic state.
proposals.method: We will adapt commercially available sterile microdialysis probes (e.g., CMA Microdialysis) with a 20 kDa molecular weight cut-off membrane for soil implantation. Probes will be embedded in zinc-contaminated soil microcosms inoculated with the metal-transforming fungus *Aspergillus niger* or a defined fungal community. A sterile perfusion fluid (e.g., artificial soil solution) will be pumped through the probe at 1 µL/min, and dialysate collected hourly over 72 hours. Solubilized zinc in dialysate will be quantified via ICP-MS. Parallel microcosms will be destructively harvested for fungal community analysis (ITS amplicon sequencing) and bulk zinc extraction to validate microdialysis measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel methodological framework for the non-destructive, spatiotemporal monitoring of microbial metal transformations in situ. It will provide unprecedented real-time data on fungal zinc solubilization kinetics directly within the soil environment, bridging a critical gap between laboratory assays and field-scale processes. The findings will advance the field of geomicrobiology by enabling direct linkage between microbial activity, community dynamics, and biogeochemical metal cycling.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct adaptation of a neuroscientific in vivo sampling technique to soil geomicrobiology, allowing for dynamic, minimally invasive measurement of microbial metabolic products in an opaque, complex solid matrix—a significant departure from destructive endpoint analyses.

proposals.title: Evolutionary Fitness of a Horizontally Acquired Zinc Solubilization Pathway in Plants: Performance in Complex Soil Matrices and Microbial Community Interactions
proposals.motivation: The discovery of bacterial-like zinc solubilization genes in plants via horizontal gene transfer (HGT) suggests a novel evolutionary adaptation for nutrient acquisition in metal-rich environments. However, the functional stability and fitness advantage conferred by this pathway have only been tested in simplified laboratory media, ignoring the complex physicochemical and biological interactions of real soil. Understanding if this HGT event provides a true competitive edge in ecologically realistic, contaminated matrices is crucial for validating its evolutionary origin and assessing its potential for enhancing phytoremediation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the horizontally acquired zinc solubilization pathway provides a measurable fitness advantage to plants in complex, zinc-contaminated soil matrices, and that this advantage is linked to specific alterations in rhizosphere microbial community structure and function.
proposals.method: We will use isogenic plant lines (e.g., Arabidopsis or a model grass) engineered to express or lack the bacterial-like zinc solubilization operon. These will be transplanted into replicated mesocosms containing authentic, characterized zinc-contaminated soil and anaerobically digested sludge. Plant growth, zinc uptake, and physiological stress markers will be quantified. Rhizosphere and bulk soil microbial communities will be analyzed via high-throughput 16S/ITS rRNA gene amplicon sequencing and metagenomics to assess diversity, structure, and functional gene shifts. Soil solution chemistry (pH, organic acids, bioavailable zinc) will be monitored over time to link plant performance to pathway activity in situ.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct test of the evolutionary fitness rationale for a plant HGT event in an ecologically relevant context. It will bridge molecular evolutionary biology with applied geomycology and phytoremediation by demonstrating how an acquired trait functions within a complex soil matrix. The results will reveal critical interactions between the engineered pathway and indigenous soil microbes, informing the design of more effective plant-based remediation systems.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond confirming gene presence or function in sterile conditions to testing the evolutionary success hypothesis in the heterogeneous environmental context where selection actually operates. It uniquely integrates plant molecular genetics with soil microbial ecology and geochemistry to deconstruct the fitness landscape of a horizontally acquired trait.

proposals.title: Spatial and single-cell transcriptomics reveal the functional architecture of fungal-bacterial consortia in zinc mineral solubilization
proposals.motivation: Fungal-bacterial communities are central to mineral weathering and metal biogeochemistry, yet bulk 'omics approaches obscure the functional contributions of individual taxa and their spatial interactions. Pre-2026 work, including Gadd's on fungal cerium and struvite biomineralization, highlights the importance of community diversity and spatial organization in mineral transformations. Understanding the specific genetic programs and microbial interactions driving zinc solubilization is critical for advancing bioremediation and biomining technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that zinc solubilization by a fungal-dominated consortium is a spatially organized process, where specific microbial taxa and metabolic cell states, identifiable via their transcriptomic profiles, perform distinct and cooperative functions at the mineral-biofilm interface.
proposals.method: We will cultivate a defined fungal-bacterial consortium, including known zinc-solubilizing fungi from Gadd's research, on insoluble zinc oxide. For spatial mapping, biofilm-mineral sections will be analyzed using the 10x Genomics Visium platform (pre-2026 established method). For single-cell resolution, dissociated consortium cells will be processed using 10x Genomics Chromium. Bioinformatic analysis will employ Cell Ranger for alignment, Seurat for integration and clustering, and spatialDE or SPOTlight for deconvoluting cell types and mapping them to spatial locations, identifying zonation of key functional genes.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first spatially resolved genetic map of a metal-solubilizing microbial community, directly linking taxonomy, gene expression, and function at the mineral interface. It will identify key fungal-bacterial interactions and novel genetic targets for community engineering, advancing fundamental geomicrobiology and applied microbial biotechnology.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting cutting-edge spatial transcriptomic tools from cancer biology to a geomicrobial system, moving beyond bulk community analysis to deconvolute the functional roles of consortium members in situ, a significant leap from pre-2026 methods that showed community diversity but could not resolve its functional architecture.

proposals.title: Fungal Transcriptional Dynamics and Real-Time Zinc Speciation Uncover Genetic Drivers of Zinc Oxide Solubilization
proposals.motivation: Fungal bioweathering of insoluble metal minerals is critical for geochemical cycling, bioremediation, and fungal nutrient acquisition. However, pre-2026 research often identifies potential solubilization genes (e.g., for reductases, organic acids) without directly linking their expression to kinetic metal release phases. This disconnect limits our ability to distinguish primary solubilization mechanisms from secondary stress responses, hindering the rational use of fungi in metal recovery or soil decontamination.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific transcriptional modules in metal-transforming fungi, particularly those involving membrane-associated reductases and metabolite exporters, will show temporally precise upregulation that directly precedes and correlates with measurable bursts of zinc ion release from ZnO.
proposals.method: The model fungus *Aspergillus niger* will be cultured with zinc oxide (ZnO) as the sole zinc source. A time-course experiment will collect synchronized samples for: 1) Total RNA extraction and Illumina RNA-seq, with differential expression analysis using DESeq2 to identify gene clusters; and 2) Culture filtrate analysis by Graphite Furnace Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (GF-AAS) for ultra-sensitive Zn²⁺ quantification. Cross-correlation analysis will link transcriptional peaks to solubilization kinetics. Candidate genes (e.g., *fre* reductase family, oxalate biosynthesis genes) will be prioritized for future knockout validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a functional genomics pipeline that directly couples gene expression to physiological function in metal solubilization. It will provide a validated set of genetic targets essential for zinc mobilization, advancing predictive models of fungal bioweathering. The findings will inform strategies for harnessing fungal communities in sustainable metal recovery and phytoremediation.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of high-resolution transcriptomics with real-time, ultra-sensitive metal speciation kinetics moves beyond static 'presence/absence' gene lists to dynamically assign physiological roles, a significant advance over pre-2026 correlative studies.

proposals.title: Zinc as a Regulatory Cofactor in the Arsenic Sequestration Pathway of Pteris vittata: A Quantitative Metallobiochemical Model
proposals.motivation: The hyperaccumulating fern Pteris vittata is a key model for arsenic phytoremediation, yet the factors controlling the flux and efficiency of its sequestration pathway are not fully understood. Pre-2026 research has detailed the roles of arsenate reductase (AR) and phytochelatin (PC) synthase but has not quantitatively linked their activity to the bioavailability of essential metal cofactors like zinc. This gap limits our ability to optimize plant performance in contaminated soils where trace metal nutrition is variable.
proposals.hypothesis: Zinc availability is a limiting factor controlling the flux through the arsenic sequestration pathway in P. vittata, acting via its role as a cofactor for key enzymes, and dynamic changes in labile zinc pools will correlate directly with pathway enzyme activity and expression.
proposals.method: We will cultivate P. vittata under controlled zinc and arsenic regimes. Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS) with microwave-assisted acid digestion will be used to quantify total zinc, while a modified extraction with a non-denaturing buffer will measure the soluble, potentially bioavailable zinc pool in roots and fronds. These measurements will be temporally correlated with enzyme activity assays for AR and PC synthase, and with qPCR analysis of corresponding gene expression (e.g., PvACR2). Statistical modeling (multiple linear regression and path analysis) will be used to establish quantitative relationships between zinc status, enzyme activity, and arsenic sequestration efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative model linking trace metal cofactor dynamics to the biochemical flux of arsenic sequestration. It will identify whether zinc nutrition is a critical, overlooked variable for optimizing P. vittata in phytoremediation. The integrated metallo-biochemical approach will provide a novel framework for understanding metal homeostasis in hyperaccumulators.
proposals.novelty: While the pathway enzymes are known, the explicit quantification of zinc as a regulatory cofactor and its dynamic relationship with pathway flux is unexplored. Fusing precise AAS-based metal analytics with enzymatic and molecular profiling to build a predictive model represents a novel, systems-level approach to phytoremediation biochemistry.

proposals.title: Fungal Community Engineering for Enhanced Zinc Bioleaching in Stirred-Tank Bioreactors: Integrating Mineral Formation and Cerium Oxide Interactions
proposals.motivation: Conventional zinc extraction from low-grade ores and industrial wastes is energy-intensive and environmentally damaging. Fungal bioleaching offers a sustainable alternative, but its translation from laboratory-scale plate or flask cultures to industrially relevant, controlled bioreactor systems remains a significant bottleneck. Scaling up is critical for process viability and requires understanding how complex microbial communities, rather than single isolates, function under dynamic bioreactor conditions to enhance leaching efficiency and mineral byproduct formation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a defined, synergistic fungal community, optimized for organic acid production and tolerance to metal toxicity, will significantly outperform axenic cultures in zinc bioleaching efficiency within a stirred-tank bioreactor. Furthermore, we posit that the addition of cerium oxide nanoparticles will modulate community dynamics and metabolic activity, leading to stabilized performance and potentially valuable secondary mineral formations like zinc oxalate or sulfate.
proposals.method: The project will employ a staged, bioreactor-based methodology. First, fungal strains (e.g., *Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*) from the Gadd lab repository, pre-screened for zinc tolerance and acid production, will be co-cultured in shake flasks to assess community synergy. The most promising consortium will be transferred to a 5L stirred-tank bioreactor with a controlled pH and aeration system, using a crushed zinc-containing industrial waste (e.g., filter cake) as substrate. Process parameters (agitation, aeration, feed rate) will be optimized using a design-of-experiments (DoE) approach. Parallel bioreactor runs will assess the impact of cerium oxide nanoparticles on community diversity (via ITS amplicon sequencing), leaching kinetics (via ICP-OES of Zn in leachate), and secondary mineral formation (via XRD and SEM-EDS).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a proven, scaled-up bioreactor protocol for fungal zinc bioleaching, moving the field beyond batch flask experiments. It will provide fundamental insights into how synthetic fungal communities maintain stability and function under industrial simulation, and how engineered nanomaterials like ceria can be used to fine-tune biogeochemical processes. The research will also identify any valuable mineral byproducts formed during the process, adding a potential resource recovery angle.
proposals.novelty: While fungal bioleaching is known, the deliberate engineering and testing of defined fungal consortia within controlled bioreactors for zinc recovery is novel. Integrating the study of cerium oxide interactions, informed by Gadd's prior work on cerium biomineralization, to potentially steer community function and mineralogy in a bioreactor system represents a unique and innovative cross-disciplinary approach.

proposals.title: Fungal Community-Mediated Solubilization of Zinc Phosphate for Dual Nutrient Release: A Biogeochemical and Microbial Diversity Study
proposals.motivation: Phosphate and zinc are critical, yet often co-limiting, micronutrients for plant growth, with many agricultural soils containing insoluble mineral reserves like zinc phosphate (hopeite). Conventional phosphate fertilizers are inefficient and environmentally damaging. Pre-2026 research by Gadd and others has established fungi as key agents in mineral weathering and phosphate solubilization, but targeted strategies for the simultaneous release of both phosphate and zinc from a single mineral source remain underexplored, offering a sustainable nutrient management solution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific fungal communities, particularly those containing phosphate-solubilizing and organic acid-producing strains, will synergistically enhance the dissolution of zinc phosphate, releasing bioavailable phosphate and zinc ions concurrently, and that this process will be influenced by community diversity and mineralogical transformations.
proposals.method: We will employ a defined experimental system using the mineral hopeite (Zn3(PO4)2·4H2O) as the sole P/Zn source. Fungal communities of varying diversity, constructed from known solubilizing strains (e.g., Aspergillus, Penicillium) and isolates from Gadd's pre-2026 work on fungal biomineralization, will be incubated with the mineral. Solubilization will be quantified via ICP-MS analysis of Zn2+ and PO43- in solution and pH monitoring. Community dynamics will be tracked using ITS amplicon sequencing. Mineral surface alterations and potential secondary mineral formation (e.g., struvite, cerium phosphate analogs for tracing) will be characterized using SEM-EDS and XRD.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic understanding of how fungal community composition drives the coupled biogeochemical cycling of phosphorus and zinc. It will deliver a proof-of-concept for a novel, microbially mediated strategy for dual-nutrient fertilizer production from mineral wastes. The findings will advance the field of fungal bioweathering and applied geomicrobiology for sustainable agriculture.
proposals.novelty: While fungal phosphate solubilization is known, the targeted dissolution of a phosphate mineral to co-release a tightly bound micronutrient (Zn) is a novel, integrated approach. The explicit focus on community-level effects on hopeite dissolution, and the potential for directed formation of valuable secondary minerals like struvite, moves beyond single-strain studies.

proposals.title: Synergistic fungal-bacterial consortia for the co-remediation of cerium and organic co-contaminants: a community engineering approach informed by mineral formation dynamics
proposals.motivation: Industrial sites are frequently contaminated with complex mixtures of heavy metals, like rare earth elements (e.g., cerium), and organic pollutants, posing a significant environmental challenge. Conventional single-organism or single-process bioremediation strategies often fail due to toxicity, metabolic incompatibility, or the inability to handle multiple contaminants simultaneously. There is a critical pre-2026 need to develop robust, multi-functional microbial systems that can detoxify such complex waste streams effectively.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a designed consortium, featuring fungi known for metal biomineralization (e.g., via oxalate or phosphate excretion leading to struvite-like phases) and bacteria with complementary organic pollutant degradation pathways, will exhibit synergistic interactions that enhance the simultaneous removal of cerium and model organic co-contaminants compared to monocultures or simple mixtures.
proposals.method: We will first screen Gadd-group-relevant fungi (e.g., *Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*) for cerium tolerance and biomineral formation, characterizing precipitates using SEM-EDS and XRD. In parallel, we will isolate bacteria from contaminated sites for degradation of a model organic pollutant (e.g., phenol). Consortia will be designed using a combinatorial assembly approach, where fungal-bacterial pairs are co-cultured in dual-contaminant media. Community dynamics and function will be tracked via qPCR for key functional genes, amplicon sequencing (16S/ITS) to assess diversity and stability, and chemical analysis of contaminant removal. The role of fungal exudates in shaping bacterial community structure and facilitating cerium immobilization (e.g., as cerium oxalate or phosphate) will be a key focus.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a proof-of-concept for rationally engineering fungal-bacterial consortia for complex pollutant mixtures. It will advance fundamental understanding of cross-kingdom microbial interactions in contaminated environments, specifically how fungal biomineralization processes influence bacterial community assembly and metabolic function. The project will generate a novel, tractable model system for studying and applying synergistic bioremediation.
proposals.novelty: While fungal metal biomineralization and bacterial degradation are well-studied in isolation, the intentional engineering of a cross-kingdom consortium where fungal excreted minerals (potentially including struvite-related phases) directly influence the bacterial degradation of a co-contaminant is a novel, systems-level approach to bioremediation. This moves beyond additive effects to target emergent, synergistic interactions.

proposals.title: Synthetic fungal metalloproteins: computational design and expression for enhanced rare earth element (Ce) and phosphate biomineralization
proposals.motivation: Fungal biomineralization, including struvite and cerium phosphate formation, is a key biogeochemical process with applications in bioremediation and element recovery. However, natural fungal communities exhibit variable and often suboptimal metal-binding affinities. Pre-2026 advances in protein design tools like Rosetta and AlphaFold2 now enable the *de novo* engineering of proteins with tailored functions, offering a route to overcome these biological limitations and create more efficient and predictable biomineralization systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that computationally designed, high-affinity metal-binding protein motifs, expressed on fungal cell surfaces, will significantly enhance the specific sequestration and biomineralization of target ions like cerium (Ce³⁺) and phosphate, compared to wild-type systems.
proposals.method: We will use the RosettaDesign framework and structural templates from known metalloproteins (e.g., lanthanide-binding tags, phytochelatins) to generate novel protein variants with optimized coordination geometries for Ce³⁺ and phosphate. Selected designs will be synthesized and fused to the N-terminus of the *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* a-agglutinin adhesion receptor (Aga1p-Aga2p system) for surface display. Engineered and control strains will be exposed to solutions containing Ce³⁺ and phosphate, with metal uptake quantified via ICP-MS and biomineral formation (e.g., cerium phosphate) characterized using SEM-EDX and XRD.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a synthetic biology pipeline for creating fungi with bespoke metal-binding surfaces, directly linking computational protein design to functional biomineralization outcomes. It will provide fundamental insights into how protein-level engineering can control mineral nucleation and advance the development of fungal-based platforms for critical element recovery and contaminated water treatment.
proposals.novelty: While fungal biomineralization is well-studied, the application of *de novo* computational protein design to create and functionally express novel metal-binding domains on fungal surfaces for targeted mineral formation is a novel, rational engineering approach beyond traditional community or physiological manipulation.

proposals.title: Electrochemically Stimulated Fungal Communities for Directed Biomineralization of Cerium and Struvite
proposals.motivation: The ability of fungi to transform metals and minerals is a cornerstone of biogeochemistry and bioremediation, but controlling these processes in complex communities remains a challenge. Pre-2026 work by Gadd and others has shown fungal roles in cerium biomineralization and struvite formation, yet methods to direct these activities are largely chemical or nutrient-based. Electrochemical methods offer precise, tunable control over redox conditions, a key driver of microbial metal transformations, but their application to fungal-dominated systems is underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying specific cathodic potentials to a fungal community will selectively stimulate the metabolic activity of metal-reducing fungi, directing the reductive precipitation of cerium oxides, while anodic potentials will promote oxidative processes and conditions favorable for struvite formation.
proposals.method: We will construct three-electrode electrochemical reactors inoculated with a defined fungal community (including known metal-transforming species from Gadd's work). Using potentiostatic control, we will apply a range of defined cathodic and anodic potentials. The geochemical evolution (pH, Eh, ion concentration) and community response (via amplicon sequencing of the ITS region) will be monitored over time. The resulting solid phases will be characterized using SEM-EDS and XRD to identify and quantify the formation of cerium minerals (e.g., cerianite) and struvite.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel methodology for the electrochemical steering of fungal biomineralization, moving from observation to direct control. It will provide fundamental insights into how redox potential shapes fungal community structure and function, and deliver a proof-of-concept for using applied potentials to produce specific mineral phases (Ce-minerals, struvite) from mixed metal/nutrient solutions.
proposals.novelty: While electro-stimulation of bacterial metal reduction is known, its targeted application to fungal communities for directing biomineralization outcomes is novel. The integration of electrochemical control with fungal community analysis to link applied potential to specific mineral products (cerium oxides vs. struvite) represents a significant advance over passive observation studies.

proposals.title: Engineering Fungal-Mineral Composites: Structured Porous Bioreactors for Cerium and Phosphate Recovery
proposals.motivation: The recovery of critical elements like rare earths (e.g., cerium) and nutrients (e.g., phosphate) from waste streams is a pressing environmental and economic challenge. Pre-2026 research shows fungi can transform metals and precipitate minerals like struvite, but these processes are often inefficient in free-cell systems. Engineered porous materials offer a platform to structure microbial communities for enhanced function and stability in flow-through systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That incorporating metal-transforming fungi (e.g., *Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*) into designed, macro-porous ceramic or polymer scaffolds will create structured bio-hybrid composites with superior and more predictable bioremediation performance for cerium immobilization and phosphate recovery as struvite, compared to free-living microbial consortia.
proposals.method: We will fabricate porous, hydrophilic ceramic (e.g., hydroxyapatite-doped) and polymer (e.g., polyurethane) scaffolds with controlled pore sizes (50-200 µm) using 3D printing and porogen leaching. These will be inoculated with fungal strains from Gadd's pre-2026 collection, known for cerium oxide nanoparticle formation and struvite precipitation. Composite function will be tested in continuous-flow columns with synthetic wastewater containing Ce(III) and phosphate. We will monitor removal efficiency, mineral phase formation (via XRD, SEM-EDS), and scaffold-associated microbial community dynamics (via 16S/ITS amplicon sequencing) over time.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for rationally designed fungal-material composites as structured bioremediation filters. It will provide new insights into how material properties (porosity, chemistry) influence fungal community development and mineral precipitation functions, moving beyond descriptive biogeochemistry towards engineered application.
proposals.novelty: While fungal biomineralization is known, the deliberate use of materials science to design the physical and chemical habitat for fungi within a macro-porous filter, specifically to control community structure and direct cerium and phosphate fate, represents a novel bio-hybrid approach distinct from simple biofilm carriers or adsorption media.

proposals.title: Fungal Community Engineering for Enhanced Silicate Bioweathering and Carbon Sequestration
proposals.motivation: Enhanced silicate weathering is a promising geoengineering strategy for atmospheric CO2 removal, but natural rates are slow. Pre-2026 research, including Gadd's work on fungal bioweathering and mineral transformations, demonstrates that fungi can accelerate silicate dissolution through acidolysis, complexolysis, and mechanical forces. However, the potential of engineered fungal communities, particularly those including mineral-precipitating fungi that can counterintuitively stimulate dissolution via metabolic coupling, remains unexplored for scalable carbon sequestration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a synthetic fungal community, combining aggressive silicate-dissolving fungi with fungi capable of secondary mineral formation (e.g., struvite, oxalates), will exhibit synergistic interactions that accelerate net silicate weathering and inorganic carbon capture compared to axenic cultures or natural assemblages.
proposals.method: We will isolate fungi from weathered silicate rock environments and select strains based on pre-2026 assays for oxalic acid production and silicate dissolution. A model community will be constructed using strains with complementary functions: 1) primary weathering agents (e.g., *Aspergillus niger*), and 2) mineral-precipitating fungi (e.g., *Metarhizium* spp. known for struvite formation). Experiments will use mesocosms containing ground basalt or olivine. Weathering rates will be quantified via solution chemistry (Si, Mg, Ca, Fe release) and pH, while carbon sequestration will be assessed via bicarbonate flux and the characterization of secondary carbonate minerals using SEM-EDS and XRD. Community dynamics will be tracked via ITS amplicon sequencing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental evidence for the concept of a 'bioweathering consortium,' directly testing if mineral precipitation processes can drive dissolution kinetics. It will deliver a mechanistic framework for community-level management of geo-biological weathering and generate concrete data on CO2 removal rates achievable under optimized fungal bioprocessing, informing future field trials.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in deliberately pairing mineral-dissolving and mineral-precipitating fungal metabolisms, a counter-intuitive approach inspired by Gadd's work on fungal fossilization and biomineralization, to create a self-sustaining, efficient bioreactor for enhanced weathering, moving beyond single-organism studies.

proposals.title: Targeted Chelation Probes for Elucidating Fungal Community Dynamics in Struvite and Rare Earth Element Biomineralization
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research shows fungi are central to biogeochemical cycles, driving mineral formation (e.g., struvite) and interacting with critical elements like cerium. However, the specific metal-binding dynamics within complex fungal communities remain opaque, limiting our ability to predict and harness these processes for bioremediation or resource recovery. Medical chelators are highly specific for target metals, offering a powerful but unexplored design logic for probing microbial systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that synthetic chelators, designed with specificity for key cations (e.g., Mg2+ in struvite, Ce3+/Ce4+), will act as targeted chemical probes to competitively disrupt and thereby illuminate the functional roles of metal-binding in fungal community structure and mineral formation dynamics.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize a library of chelators based on medical scaffolds (e.g., DTPA, deferoxamine analogs) with tailored affinity for Mg2+, NH4+, PO43-, and Ce3+/4+. These will be applied to defined fungal co-cultures, including known mineral-forming strains from Gadd's research, under conditions promoting struvite or cerium phosphate biomineralization. Community dynamics will be tracked via ITS/18S rRNA amplicon sequencing, while mineral formation and metal speciation will be analyzed using SEM-EDS, XRD, and ICP-MS. Chelator-induced perturbations in diversity, mineralogy, and metal distribution will pinpoint metal-specific functional nodes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel chelation-probe methodology for microbial ecology, directly linking specific metal pools to community function. It will provide mechanistic insight into fungal roles in environmentally relevant mineralization processes, advancing the field beyond descriptive studies towards predictive manipulation of microbial systems for metal sequestration or recovery.
proposals.novelty: While chelators are used broadly in microbiology, the deliberate design and application of medical-grade, target-specific chelators as dynamic probes for dissecting metal-mediated interactions in fungal communities is a novel conceptual and technical approach, merging clinical bioinorganic chemistry with environmental mycology.

proposals.title: Fungal Community Dynamics and Rare Earth Element Biomineralization in Single-Vine Rhizosphere Microgradients
proposals.motivation: The rhizosphere is a critical biogeochemical interface where microbial activity drives nutrient cycling and metal(loid) transformations, influencing plant health and soil remediation. However, pre-2026 understanding of these processes is largely based on bulk soil analyses, obscuring the millimeter-scale chemical and biological gradients that form around individual roots. Investigating these microgradients is essential to unravel the specific roles of fungi, known for their metal- and mineral-transforming capabilities, in localized nutrient (e.g., phosphate as struvite) and rare earth element (e.g., cerium) dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct fungal communities, structured along millimeter-scale chemical gradients from the vine root surface, are key mediators of cerium speciation and phosphate mineral (e.g., struvite) formation, with community diversity and function directly linked to the steepness of the pH and redox potential microgradients.
proposals.method: We will employ a novel micro-sampling approach using rhizoboxes and micro-suction cups to collect pore water at 1-2 mm intervals from the root surface of potted vines. High-resolution chemical mapping (pH, Eh, Ce speciation via DGT, phosphate) will be coupled with spatially resolved microbial community analysis using DNA/RNA extracted from the corresponding soil micro-cores. Fungal community composition and activity will be assessed via ITS and 18S rRNA gene amplicon sequencing, correlated with microgradient data, and linked to biomineral formation using SEM-EDS and synchrotron-based μ-XRF/XANES to identify Ce and P mineral phases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution spatial map linking fungal community structure to rare earth element and phosphate biogeochemistry in a single-plant rhizosphere. It will define the functional roles of specific fungal taxa in creating or responding to microgradients, advancing fundamental knowledge of fungal biomineralization processes. The findings will inform strategies for phytostabilization of metal-contaminated sites and precision management of vine nutrition.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, millimeter-scale coupling of fungal community analysis with cerium speciation and phosphate mineralogy in the rhizosphere of a single plant, moving beyond bulk soil studies to resolve the precise spatial scales at which these critical fungal-mediated processes occur.

proposals.title: Fungal-Mediated Coupling of Cerium Redox Transformations to Struvite Biomineralization and Community Nitrogen Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Biogeochemical cycles are often studied in isolation, yet in natural and engineered systems, metal transformations are intrinsically linked to nutrient cycles. Understanding these couplings is critical for predicting ecosystem function and designing effective bioremediation or resource recovery strategies. Pre-2026 work on fungal biomineralization, particularly of struvite (a nitrogen- and phosphorus-containing mineral), and fungal interactions with rare earth elements like cerium, provides a unique foundation to investigate these interconnected processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fungal redox transformations of cerium (Ce(III)/Ce(IV)) directly influence nitrogen availability and speciation within a microbial community, thereby regulating the kinetics and location of struvite formation. This coupling is non-independent and modulated by fungal community diversity.
proposals.method: We will establish microcosm experiments with defined fungal communities (including known struvite-forming and metal-transforming species from Gadd's prior work) in nitrogen-limited media amended with Ce(III) or Ce(IV). Using a combination of geochemical monitoring (pH, NH4+, PO43-), mineralogical analysis (XRD, SEM-EDS), and spectroscopic techniques (XANES for Ce speciation), we will track coupled Ce redox shifts and struvite precipitation dynamics. High-throughput sequencing (ITS and 16S rRNA) of the fungal and bacterial communities will be performed to correlate process rates with shifts in microbial diversity and function after mineral formation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, process-level understanding of how fungal metal transformations are coupled to a key nutrient (nitrogen) cycle and its mineralogical outcome (struvite). It will advance the field of fungal biomineralization beyond descriptive studies to a predictive framework for interdependent biogeochemical cycles. The findings will inform strategies for simultaneous metal bioremediation and nutrient recovery in engineered systems.
proposals.novelty: While fungal roles in individual cycles (metal transformation, struvite formation) are known, explicitly testing their interdependence and quantifying the role of community diversity in modulating this coupling is a novel, systems-level approach. Integrating the biogeochemistry of a rare earth element (cerium) with a agriculturally relevant mineral (struvite) is also highly innovative.

proposals.title: Hybrid fungal-physicochemical treatment for enhanced phosphate recovery and cerium immobilization from complex waste streams
proposals.motivation: The recovery of phosphate as struvite is a critical strategy for nutrient recycling, but efficiency is often limited by complex waste matrices containing competing ions and toxic metals like cerium, a rare earth element of increasing environmental concern. Pre-2026 work shows fungal communities can transform metals and minerals, but their integration with engineered physicochemical steps for targeted resource recovery remains underexplored. A hybrid system could overcome the limitations of standalone biological or chemical methods.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sequential treatment system, using a defined fungal community to pre-condition waste streams through biomineralization and biosorption, will significantly enhance the efficiency and purity of subsequent phosphate recovery via struvite precipitation and enable concurrent immobilization of cerium.
proposals.method: We will first screen and characterize fungal consortia from metal-contaminated sites for cerium tolerance and phosphate solubilization/mobilization activity. A two-stage bioreactor will be established: Stage 1 involves fungal treatment of synthetic wastewater containing phosphate, ammonium, magnesium, and cerium ions. Fungal biomass and excreted metabolites will alter speciation and promote initial cerium sequestration. The fungal-conditioned effluent will then flow to Stage 2, a controlled crystallizer where pH and Mg:N:P ratios are optimized for selective struvite formation. The final effluent and fungal biomass will be analyzed using XRD, SEM-EDS, and ICP-MS to quantify struvite yield/purity and cerium distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel process blueprint for synergistic resource recovery and contaminant stabilization, directly contributing to the circular economy. It will advance fundamental understanding of fungal-physicochemical interactions in engineered biomineralization systems, specifically for coupled phosphate and rare earth element management.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate, sequential coupling of fungal bioprocessing (for community-driven mobilization/immobilization) with targeted struvite precipitation, designed not just for treatment but to improve the quality and recoverability of both phosphate and critical metal fractions, which is non-obvious in standard single-process approaches.

proposals.title: Fungal Biomimetic Nanostructures: Engineering Metal-Binding Sites from Fungal Communities for Selective Lanthanide Adsorption
proposals.motivation: The recovery of critical rare earth elements (REEs), like cerium, from waste streams is a major environmental and economic challenge. Conventional adsorbents lack the selectivity and efficiency of biological systems. Pre-2026 work by Gadd and others has shown that fungal communities and their metabolic products (e.g., struvite formation) can control metal speciation and mineral nucleation, offering a blueprint for precise metal binding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that nanoscale materials, functionalized with biomimetic ligands derived from fungal metal-binding sites, will exhibit superior selectivity and adsorption capacity for target REEs (e.g., Ce³⁺) compared to non-biomimetic counterparts, by replicating the coordination chemistry of the native biological system.
proposals.method: We will first characterize metal-binding functional groups (e.g., phosphoryl, carboxyl, amine) in fungal mycelia and associated biominerals (e.g., struvite, oxalates) from metal-stressed communities using FTIR and XPS. These molecular motifs will inform the synthesis of silica or polymer nanoparticles (50-100 nm) via sol-gel or precipitation polymerization, with surface functionalization using selected ligands (e.g., phosphonate, citrate). Adsorption kinetics and isotherms for Ce³⁺ will be quantified using ICP-MS, with selectivity tested against competing ions (e.g., Ca²⁺, Al³⁺). Material characterization will involve TEM, BET surface area analysis, and zeta potential measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct link between fungal community metal-binding mechanisms and the rational design of advanced adsorbents. It will contribute a novel class of biomimetic nanomaterials with tunable specificity for REEs, advancing the field of biogeomimicry for resource recovery. The findings will also provide fundamental insights into how nanoscale replication of biological sites influences metal coordination.
proposals.novelty: While fungal biomining is known, the deliberate extraction of metal-binding 'design principles' from fungal communities to create synthetic nanoscale biomimetic adsorbents is a novel, bottom-up approach. The focus on replicating the precise chemical environment, rather than using whole biomass or crude extracts, for selective REE capture is a significant conceptual advance over pre-2026 biosorption studies.

proposals.title: Fungal Community Dynamics and Organic Acid Production for the Controlled Mobilization of Cerium and Other Metals from Contaminated Struvite and Mineral Matrices
proposals.motivation: While fungal biomineralization for metal immobilization is well-studied, the inverse process—controlled metal mobilization—remains underexplored despite its potential for resource recovery from secondary wastes like metal-laden struvite or remediation by-products. Understanding how to intentionally and predictably solubilize metals using fungal communities, rather than pure cultures, could unlock novel biorecycling strategies and refine our models of fungal biogeochemistry in dynamic environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, manipulable fungal community assemblages, through the production of targeted organic acids and siderophores, can be engineered to selectively and sequentially mobilize metals like cerium from mixed mineral matrices, with the mobilization efficiency and specificity being a predictable function of community diversity and metabolic output.
proposals.method: The study will employ a two-phase microcosm approach using metal-contaminated synthetic struvite and common soil minerals as substrates. First, a diverse pre-2026 fungal culture collection, including known metal-tolerant and acid-producing strains (e.g., *Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*), will be used to establish defined communities of varying diversity. Second, mobilization experiments will monitor the kinetics of cerium and co-contaminant release, correlating metal fluxes with community metabolic profiling (organic acid/siderophore quantification via HPLC/LC-MS) and dynamic shifts in community structure (ITS amplicon sequencing). Key variables include carbon source manipulation to direct acid production and the addition of specific mineral phases to test mobilization selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational framework for 'biologically programmed metal mobilization,' directly extending Gadd's work on fungal biomineralization into its functional inverse. It will generate novel data on how community-level interactions, rather than single-species activity, govern metal solubilization dynamics from complex minerals, with direct implications for designing biorecovery processes from industrial and remediation wastes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a community ecology and systems biology lens to the intentional *mobilization* of metals, a deliberate inversion of the remediation paradigm, and in targeting the controlled deconstruction of specific biomineralization products (e.g., metal-loaded struvite) rather than primary ores.

ideas.title: Assess the evolutionary fitness and functional stability of a horizontally acquired zinc solubilization pathway in plants within complex soil matrices
ideas.description: This research direction investigates whether the horizontally acquired, bacterial-like zinc solubilization mechanism in plants confers a measurable fitness advantage in authentic, contaminated soil or sludge matrices. By transplanting engineered plant lines (with and without the pathway) into complex field-relevant matrices and monitoring their growth, zinc uptake, and microbial community interactions, the study directly tests the evolutionary origin hypothesis in an ecologically realistic context. This approach reveals if the pathway's evolutionary success is linked to its performance in heterogeneous soil environments, where factors like pH, organic matter, and competing microbes are present, which cannot be replicated in simplified lab media. The synergy lies in using real-world matrix performance as a direct assay for the pathway's evolutionary rationale, bridging molecular evolution with applied phytoremediation potential.

ideas.title: Quantify Zinc Cofactor Dynamics in the Arsenic Sequestration Pathway Using Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy
ideas.description: This research fuses precise atomic absorption spectroscopy for zinc quantification with the biochemical mapping of the arsenic sequestration pathway in the fern Pteris vittata. The novel direction investigates the hypothesis that zinc, as a cofactor for key enzymes like arsenate reductase and phytochelatin synthase, is a limiting factor controlling pathway flux and efficiency. By correlating real-time, solubilized zinc concentrations in tissue compartments with the activity and expression of pathway enzymes, the study will establish a quantitative metallo-biochemical model. This approach reveals not just the pathway's structure, but its dynamic regulation by trace metal nutrition, which is critical for optimizing phytoremediation strategies.

ideas.title: Controlled Solubilization: A Targeted Delivery Approach for Zinc Bioremediation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that solubilizing toxic zinc is always beneficial for bioremediation by drawing an analogy from pharmaceutical drug delivery. In that field, uncontrolled solubility leads to toxicity, solved by targeted delivery systems. Adapted to bioremediation, the research idea proposes developing controlled solubilization strategies. These strategies would use spatially or chemically constrained release of solubilizing agents to create high-bioavailability zones specifically for a tailored microbial consortium capable of immobilization or degradation, thereby maximizing remediation efficiency while minimizing ecological and toxicological risks from mobile zinc.

ideas.title: Multivariate deconvolution of organic acid profiles and chelator contributions to phosphate solubilization dynamics
ideas.description: This redesigned study moves beyond a single biomarker to simultaneously quantify a panel of organic acids and chelators commonly associated with microbial phosphate solubilization using HPLC-MS/MS. The production dynamics of each compound are tracked over time and correlated with real-time measurements of phosphate solubilization efficiency and pH. Statistical modeling, including principal component regression and variance partitioning, is then applied to determine the individual and synergistic contributions of each metabolite to the overall solubilization process, identifying the key drivers and potential inhibitory interactions.

ideas.title: Comparative functional profiling of airborne isolates to map phylogenetic and substrate-specific solubilization strategies
ideas.description: This redesigned study systematically characterizes the nine untested airborne isolates not in isolation, but within a comparative framework that groups them by phylogeny (e.g., 16S rRNA gene-based clades) and tests their solubilization capabilities against a defined gradient of environmentally relevant, insoluble substrates (e.g., tricalcium phosphate, ferrihydrite, crystalline cellulose). By measuring solubilization kinetics and efficiency across this matrix, the work moves beyond a strain-by-strain list to identify conserved functional traits, substrate preferences, and potential mechanistic strategies (e.g., acidification, siderophore production, enzymatic hydrolysis) associated with specific taxonomic groups. This approach transforms the data into a predictive map of solubilization ecology, generating hypotheses about the evolutionary drivers and environmental niches of these airborne microbes.

ideas.title: Assess zinc solubilization in soil using a microdialysis probe adapted from neuroscience
ideas.description: This idea adapts the in vivo microdialysis technique from neuroscience to assess a bacterium's zinc solubilization activity in complex, real-world matrices like contaminated soil or sludge. The analogous neuroscience solution involves implanting a semi-permeable membrane probe into brain tissue to dynamically sample extracellular fluid, measuring analytes in a living, complex system. The adapted research idea embeds a sterile microdialysis probe with an appropriate membrane into the soil or sludge inoculated with the bacterium. By perfusing the probe and collecting the dialysate over a time course, researchers can obtain real-time, spatially resolved measurements of solubilized zinc concentrations, directly quantifying the microbial activity within the intact, heterogeneous matrix without the need for destructive extraction methods.

ideas.title: Directed Evolution of Arsenic-Tolerant Biosensors for Real-Time Optimization of Phytoremediation
ideas.description: This research fuses directed evolution with biosensor technology to develop a self-optimizing bioremediation platform. Zinc-responsive biosensor bacteria are engineered to also express arsenic tolerance genes, then subjected to directed evolution under dual arsenic and zinc stress. The evolved strains function as 'smart' microbial agents that not only report real-time bioavailable zinc levels via bioluminescence but also possess adaptively enhanced arsenic sequestration or detoxification capabilities. This creates a dynamic system where the biosensor signal guides the application of amendments, while the evolved microbial community actively and adaptively remediates co-contaminated soils.

ideas.title: Employ transcriptomics and spatial mapping to identify genes and cell types involved in zinc solubilization
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from cancer biology's use of spatial and single-cell transcriptomics to deconvolute the tumor microenvironment, this idea adapts that solution to microbial systems. Instead of using bulk RNA-seq on a culture growing on insoluble zinc, the adapted approach employs spatial transcriptomics (e.g., on a biofilm attached to a zinc mineral) or single-cell RNA-seq of a microbial consortium. This will identify not just upregulated genes, but will map them to specific microbial taxa or metabolic cell states, revealing the functional architecture of the zinc-solubilizing community and the genetic basis of interactions between members.

ideas.title: Engineer a synthetic arsenic sequestration pathway in plants using a biomimetic organelle
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from synthetic biology approaches in microbial bioremediation, where complete pathways are engineered to capture, reduce, and sequester heavy metals into protected cellular compartments, this idea adapts that solution for crop plants. The research would involve designing a synthetic genetic module that recreates this multi-step bacterial-like tolerance mechanism within a plant cellular context. The module would be engineered to express a suite of proteins that work in concert: first, to bind arsenic with high specificity; second, to reduce it to a less mobile form (arsenite); and third, to actively pump and trap it within the plant vacuole, a natural storage organelle. This creates a biomimetic 'detoxification organelle' that isolates arsenic from the plant's metabolism and, crucially, from the edible portions of the crop, aiming for safer food production from contaminated soils.

ideas.title: Cross-Metalloid Biosensor for Specificity Profiling of Tolerance Mechanisms
ideas.description: This research fuses the specificity question from Idea A with the biosensor quantification approach from Idea B. It involves engineering a suite of biosensor bacterial strains, each responsive to a different toxic metalloid (e.g., arsenic, antimony, zinc), and introducing into them the arsenic tolerance mechanism under investigation. By exposing these strains to mixed metalloid environments and monitoring their real-time biosensor output, the research can simultaneously quantify the intracellular bioavailability of each metalloid in the presence of the mechanism. This allows for direct, competitive assessment of whether the mechanism selectively excludes arsenic or confers broad-spectrum tolerance to multiple elements, a question difficult to resolve with traditional growth assays.

ideas.title: Coupling Transcriptomic Profiling with Real-Time Zinc Speciation to Decipher Microbial Solubilization Mechanisms
ideas.description: This integrated approach combines RNA-seq analysis with atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) in a time-course experiment where microbial cultures are grown on insoluble zinc sources. By taking synchronized samples for both transcriptomics and ultra-sensitive zinc quantification, the research can link specific transcriptional bursts—such as the upregulation of candidate reductase, chelator, or acidification genes—to measurable phases of zinc ion release into solution. This creates a powerful functional genomics pipeline that moves beyond mere gene identification to validate the physiological role of upregulated genes in the actual solubilization process, distinguishing primary actors from secondary stress responses.

ideas.title: Engineering a Metabolic Niche: Finding Superior Carbon Sources Through Directed Evolution and Co-cultivation
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that glucose is the optimal carbon source by adapting a solution from microbial ecology and bioremediation. In those fields, the optimality of a single substrate or degrader was overturned by using enrichment cultures and directed evolution within complex substrate mixtures to discover more efficient alternatives. Adapted to this context, the idea is to not merely compare known sugars to glucose, but to set up a discovery platform. By subjecting diverse microbial consortia or genetic libraries to serial cultivation in minimal media containing complex, undefined carbon mixtures (like plant biomass hydrolysates), we apply strong selective pressure for growth. This directed evolution in a community setting is designed to force the emergence of strains or consortia that have evolved to utilize components of the mixture more efficiently than glucose, thereby systematically uncovering novel, superior carbon sources and the metabolic networks that make them optimal under defined selective conditions.

ideas.title: Testing the environmental function of airborne bacteria by challenging them with hyperaccumulator root exudates
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to isolate bacteria from the air surrounding various hyperaccumulator plants (e.g., Arabidopsis halleri, Noccaea caerulescens) and directly challenge their purported environmental function. The core experiment is to expose these airborne isolates to the root exudates and tissue extracts of their source hyperaccumulator, testing for specific tolerance or growth promotion in the presence of high concentrations of the accumulated metal (e.g., arsenic, cadmium, nickel). This approach synergistically tests Idea B's contamination hypothesis by demanding a functional, metal-specific response, while simultaneously expanding Idea A's search for unique mechanisms beyond Pteris vittata by using functional screening as the discovery tool rather than genetic homology.

ideas.title: Quantify 2-ketogluconic acid production using a chemostat-coupled biosensor for real-time correlation with solubilization dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from environmental engineering, where chemostats coupled with inline biosensors are used for real-time monitoring of specific metabolites to correlate with process efficiency. The analogous problem of monitoring volatile fatty acids in wastewater to control treatment processes is solved using continuous cultivation and real-time sensing. Adapted to the original research context, the idea is to cultivate phosphate-solubilizing bacteria in a chemostat system integrated with a biosensor specific for 2-ketogluconic acid. This setup enables the continuous, direct quantification of the target acid, allowing its production to be dynamically correlated with real-time measurements of pH drop and phosphate solubilization efficiency in the effluent, moving beyond single time-point assays to capture system dynamics.

ideas.title: Engineer a Synthetic Gene Circuit for Tunable Arsenic Biosensing and Sequestration
ideas.description: This research fuses the concepts of biosensor engineering and hyperaccumulator genetics by using the identified bacterial-like tolerance genes from Pteris vittata as functional modules within a synthetic gene circuit. The circuit is designed to be arsenic-concentration-dependent: at low, sub-toxic arsenic levels, it expresses a reporter gene (e.g., for visible color change) to act as an early-warning biosensor. Upon detecting higher, hazardous concentrations, the circuit triggers the expression of the full hyperaccumulation and sequestration gene suite, converting the plant from a passive sensor into an active phytoremediator. This creates a smart, dual-phase plant technology for both monitoring and managing soil arsenic.

ideas.title: Systematic Phenotypic Profiling of Nine Airborne Isolates Using a High-Throughput Mineral Screening Array
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from clinical microbiology's use of standardized phenotypic panels (e.g., VITEK 2, API strips) for pathogen characterization, this idea adapts that high-throughput, parallel testing solution. The analogous solution involves culturing a single isolate against an array of many biochemical substrates to generate a metabolic profile. Adapted here, the nine untested airborne isolates will each be screened against a standardized array of diverse insoluble mineral compounds (e.g., different phosphate forms, silicates) solidified in culture media. This method will efficiently generate a comparative phenotypic profile for each isolate, characterizing not just if they solubilize, but their specific mineral preferences, solubilization efficiency, and potential niche-specific adaptations, moving beyond a binary assessment to a multidimensional functional characterization.

ideas.title: Evaluating the fitness cost of a specific metalloid tolerance mechanism in a multi-contaminant environment
ideas.description: This study investigates whether the absence of a fitness cost for arsenic hyperaccumulation is a direct consequence of the tolerance mechanism's specificity for arsenic alone. We hypothesize that if the mechanism does not confer cross-tolerance to other metalloids like antimony, the plant avoids the energetic and physiological burdens of a generalized defense system, explaining the lack of growth or reproductive trade-offs. The research will simultaneously measure fitness parameters and tolerance to arsenic and antimony in controlled environments to correlate mechanistic specificity with fitness outcomes. This approach uniquely tests if evolutionary fitness in contaminated soils is optimized by highly specific, rather than broad-spectrum, detoxification pathways.

ideas.title: High-throughput cross-tolerance profiling to define the specificity and pleiotropy of a putative arsenic resistance mechanism
ideas.description: This study first uses a high-throughput growth assay in a genetically tractable model organism (e.g., yeast or bacteria) expressing the arsenic tolerance mechanism, testing it against a panel of diverse toxicants including antimony, cadmium, chromium, selenium, and mercury. This phenotypic screen identifies the full spectrum of conferred resistance. Subsequent genetic and biochemical analyses then determine if tolerance to non-arsenic compounds is due to direct detoxification by the same mechanism, indirect pleiotropic effects (e.g., altered general stress response or membrane transport), or is merely an artifact of shared chemical properties. This approach definitively maps the mechanism's functional scope and evolutionary implications beyond a single element.

ideas.title: Airborne Microbial Contaminants as Bioengineered Sentinels for Soil Toxin Drift
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that airborne bacteria are mere contaminants by deliberately engineering plant-associated bacteria to be hyper-sensitive to soil toxins like arsenic. These engineered microbes are then aerosolized and released to act as transient, living environmental sensors, contaminating the phyllosphere of plants across a landscape. When these airborne 'contaminant' microbes encounter arsenic dust or volatiles deposited from contaminated soil, they trigger a detectable phenotypic response in their host plants. This transforms airborne microbial ecology from a passive contamination concern into an active, deployable biosurveillance network for mapping toxic drift from soil.

ideas.title: Correlative XRF-XANES imaging for arsenic speciation and localization at cellular resolution
ideas.description: This redesigned system uses a synchrotron microprobe to perform raster-scan X-ray fluorescence imaging, generating a high-resolution map of total arsenic distribution. At each pixel or regions of high arsenic concentration, a full X-ray absorption spectrum is collected to perform XANES analysis, fingerprinting the local arsenic speciation (e.g., arsenate, arsenite, or bound to sulfur or organic molecules). This correlative dataset directly links spatial localization with biochemical state, distinguishing between detoxified storage vacuoles and metabolically active toxic species. The approach overcomes the critical limitation of elemental-only mapping by revealing the functional chemistry driving arsenic toxicity and tolerance in biological tissues.

ideas.title: Dynamic X-ray Fluorescence-Guided Engineering of Arsenic-Hyperaccumulating Microalgae
ideas.description: This research fuses synthetic biology with advanced analytical chemistry to engineer arsenic-removing microalgae in an iterative, data-driven loop. Engineered algal strains with novel arsenic tolerance or sequestration pathways are exposed to contaminated water, and their performance is analyzed not just by bulk removal, but by using synchrotron-based X-ray fluorescence (XRF) imaging to map arsenic localization at subcellular resolution in living cells. The high-resolution spatial data—showing whether arsenic is safely compartmentalized in vacuoles, bound to specific proteins, or causing toxic dispersion—directly feeds back to re-engineer the algal chassis, optimizing genetic constructs for ideal intracellular trafficking and storage. This synergy enables the rational design of hyperaccumulating biocatalysts by visually linking genetic modifications to their precise functional outcome in situ, moving beyond trial-and-error to mechanism-informed strain development.

ideas.title: High-resolution ICP-MS with interference removal for ultra-trace zinc speciation and quantification in complex matrices
ideas.description: This redesigned approach employs inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) equipped with a collision/reaction cell to remove polyatomic interferences that plague atomic absorption methods. It incorporates a multi-internal standard calibration protocol to correct for matrix-induced signal suppression and instrumental drift, ensuring accuracy in complex samples like serum, plant digests, or wastewater. The method provides exceptional sensitivity for quantifying zinc at ultra-trace levels and can be coupled with chromatography to differentiate between labile and protein-bound zinc species. This results in a far more robust, interference-resistant, and information-rich analytical system compared to standalone atomic absorption spectroscopy.

ideas.title: Rhizosphere Sentinel: Engineered plants that excrete pigment upon arsenic detection
ideas.description: This approach engineers a model plant (e.g., *Arabidopsis* or a rapid-cycling brassica) with a synthetic arsenic-responsive genetic circuit linked to the production and root-secreted excretion of a stable, visible pigment. The system is designed to activate at arsenic concentrations below regulatory safety limits for agriculture. The pigment stains the soil around the sentinel plant's roots, providing an unambiguous, early-warning visual signal of contamination before food crops are planted or can accumulate arsenic, enabling proactive soil management.

ideas.title: Biofilm-resistant microfluidic chemostat with active fouling mitigation for robust kinetic studies
ideas.description: This redesigned platform combines microfluidic chemostats with integrated, real-time biofilm detection using optical coherence tomography (OCT). Upon detection of micro-fouling, a feedback-controlled system triggers localized, brief interventions—such as transient high-shear flow or pulsed introduction of biofilm-dispersing agents—specifically targeted to the fouling site. This approach maintains a stable, homogeneous planktonic culture and consistent nutrient/metabolite gradients, enabling long-duration, real-time kinetic studies of bacterial growth and acid production without data corruption from biofilm artifacts or system failure.

ideas.title: Contextual Substrate Pareto-Optimization: Mapping Carbon Source Trade-Offs for Defined Metabolic Objectives
ideas.description: This project abandons the search for a universally 'superior' carbon source and instead creates a systematic framework to identify conditionally optimal substrates. It uses a combination of computational modeling and experimental validation to map how different substrates (e.g., glycerol, acetate, xylose, methanol) perform across multiple, often competing, metabolic objectives like growth rate, yield, and product-specific precursor flux. The output is a publicly accessible 'Substrate Performance Atlas' that defines the Pareto-optimal front for various industrial microbes and target products, enabling rational substrate selection based on the specific priorities of a bioprocess.

ideas.title: Engineered Biosensors Validated by Synchrotron Imaging for High-Throughput Arsenic Phenotyping
ideas.description: This research fuses the creation of arsenic-hypersensitive plant biosensors with synchrotron-based X-ray fluorescence (XRF) imaging to establish a novel phenotyping and validation pipeline. The engineered plants serve as rapid, visible indicators of soil arsenic, while cellular-resolution XRF mapping precisely quantifies arsenic uptake and localization within their tissues, confirming sensor response and revealing internal sequestration patterns. This synergy allows for the high-throughput screening of different genetic constructs to optimize sensitivity and specificity, moving beyond simple detection to understanding the physiological mechanisms of hyper-accumulation or exclusion. The validated biosensor lines can then be deployed as reliable early-warning systems, with their performance fully characterized at the elemental level.

ideas.title: Apply Phage-Assisted Continuous Evolution (PACE) to pre-optimize arsenic resistance operons
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from protein engineering, where Phage-Assisted Continuous Evolution (PACE) is used to rapidly evolve novel enzyme functions in a continuous, automated bioreactor. The analogous solution links a target protein's activity to the propagation of a modified bacteriophage, enabling orders-of-magnitude faster directed evolution. Adapted to this context, a PACE system would be designed where the survival and replication of a host-specific phage in E. coli is made dependent on the efficient expression and function of an arsenic detoxification operon (e.g., arsRBAC). Arsenic concentration in the chemostat serves as the selective pressure. This setup would continuously evolve and optimize the entire operon for maximal tolerance and efflux efficiency over many bacterial generations in a matter of days. The resulting pre-optimized, multi-gene cassette, honed in a bacterial chassis, would then be a superior module for transfer and expression in plants, potentially leading to faster development of hyper-tolerant phytoremediation crops.

ideas.title: A Functional Metagenomic and Phenotypic Screen to Distinguish Atmospheric Niche Specialists from Passive Contaminants
ideas.description: This project moves beyond the simple assertion that airborne bacteria are contaminants by creating a testable framework to identify genuine atmospheric niche specialists. It employs controlled aerosolization chambers to subject diverse microbial communities to atmospheric conditions (UV, desiccation, oxidative stress) and uses multi-omics (metagenomics, metatranscriptomics, metabolomics) to track active metabolic pathways and adaptive responses. Concurrent phenotypic assays measure traits like ice-nucleation activity, UV resistance, and growth on atmospheric carbon substrates. The core innovation is a direct comparison cohort: known terrestrial and aquatic contaminants are run through the same system, providing a definitive baseline to statistically identify traits that are enriched and functionally relevant only in the atmospheric environment.

ideas.title: Applying Cosmological 'Dark Matter' Methodology to Airborne Microbiome Analysis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that airborne bacteria are merely contaminants by adapting a solution from cosmology's study of dark matter. In cosmology, dark matter's existence was proven not by direct detection but by its consistent gravitational effects on visible systems (like galaxy rotation). Analogously, this research would shift focus from direct microbial characterization to searching for the functional 'signature' of airborne bacteria within atmospheric processes. The adapted experiment would rigorously control air masses to measure whether bacterial presence and diversity predictably alter measurable atmospheric functions—such as cloud formation potential, ice nucleation efficiency, or chemical transformation rates—that cannot be explained by abiotic factors or passive contamination alone. Finding such a consistent functional correlation would serve as indirect but powerful evidence for specialized environmental roles, moving beyond the contamination debate.

ideas.title: Arsenic Detection via Synthetic Feedback-Loop Amplification
ideas.description: This idea expands the original arsenic sensor concept by applying an analogical solution from synthetic biology. The analogous problem is detecting weak molecular signals in cellular environments, solved by designing genetic circuits with positive feedback loops to amplify the signal. We adapt this by engineering a biosensor where components of the arsenic tolerance pathway are used to construct a synthetic positive feedback loop: arsenic binding activates a promoter that expresses both a fluorescent reporter and a protein that further activates the same promoter. This creates a switch-like, ultrasensitive detection system, addressing the SCALING_DUAL dimension by enhancing the signal-to-noise ratio and enabling clear binary readouts suitable for field deployment.

ideas.title: Reconstruct a tolerance pathway in yeast using a modular, plug-and-play synthetic biology framework
ideas.description: This idea adapts the modular design and assembly principles from electrical engineering circuit boards to the challenge of reconstructing a complex biological tolerance pathway in yeast. Instead of engineering the pathway as one monolithic unit, the solution involves creating a library of standardized, interoperable genetic parts (sensors, logic gates, actuators) with predictable performance. These parts are then assembled in a yeast chassis like components on a PCB, following a detailed connectivity schematic of the target pathway. This modular approach allows for stepwise construction, independent testing and optimization of sub-circuits, and easier troubleshooting, ultimately leading to a more robust and efficiently reconstructed tolerance system.

ideas.title: Evolutionary origins of plant stress tolerance via horizontal gene transfer from functional airborne bacteria
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that airborne bacteria are mere contaminants by investigating them as potential historical sources of horizontally transferred genes that conferred stress tolerance to plants. It proposes to screen contemporary airborne bacterial isolates for functional genes homologous to those in the plant's bacterial-like tolerance pathway, assessing their potential for transfer and adaptive benefit. By comparing these genes with the plant's pathway phylogenetically, the study aims to identify if the pathway's origin can be traced to ancestral atmospheric interactions. This directly tests a novel evolutionary hypothesis: that the atmosphere serves as a dynamic conduit for gene flow, seeding major adaptive innovations in terrestrial eukaryotes.

ideas.title: Phylogenomic mapping and ancestral state reconstruction of a eukaryotic stress tolerance pathway
ideas.description: This study first determines the evolutionary history of the tolerance pathway by performing a broad phylogenomic screen across diverse eukaryotic lineages to distinguish between horizontal transfer, ancient conservation, and convergent evolution. Using this resolved phylogeny, it then reconstructs the ancestral states of the pathway's key components in the last common ancestor of the plant lineage. Finally, it employs ancestral protein resurrection and functional assays in model systems to empirically test the biochemical properties and selective pressures that shaped the pathway's origin and diversification.

ideas.title: Tracing the evolutionary acquisition of a bacterial-like tolerance pathway in plants using horizontal gene transfer detection methods
ideas.description: This research idea adapts solutions from the field of bacterial comparative genomics, specifically methods developed to trace the horizontal acquisition of antibiotic resistance genes. The analogous problem—determining how a bacterial-like trait appears in a distant lineage—was solved by detecting genomic signatures of horizontal gene transfer (HGT). We propose to apply this HGT detection toolkit to investigate the evolutionary origin of a bacterial-like tolerance pathway in a eukaryotic plant lineage. The adapted research will involve screening plant genomes for the pathway and analyzing flanking regions for mobile genetic elements, assessing nucleotide composition (GC content, codon usage bias) for atypical patterns, and performing phylogenetic comparisons to test for incongruence between the pathway's gene tree and the plant species tree. This approach will robustly test the hypothesis of an ancient horizontal transfer event from bacteria into plants, clarifying the evolutionary mechanism behind this unusual pathway acquisition.

ideas.title: Dual: Causally Testing Arsenic's Defensive Role in a Multi-Trophic Context
ideas.description: This study rigorously tests whether arsenic hyperaccumulation is an evolved herbivore defense strategy by moving beyond correlation to establish causation. Using genetically similar plant lines with contrasting arsenic accumulation capabilities grown in controlled arsenic conditions, it measures herbivore preference, performance, and survival in choice and no-choice assays. To isolate arsenic's effect, plant tissues are chemically fractionated, and purified arsenic compounds are incorporated into artificial diets for insect feeding trials. The integrated design disentangles the selective advantage of arsenic accumulation from coincidental tolerance, determining if defense is a primary driver of this unusual phenotype.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Shadowing of Fern Stress Tolerance Genes
ideas.description: This idea adapts the method of 'phylogenetic shadowing' from medical virology (specifically HIV/SIV research) to plant comparative genomics. In virology, this technique compares viral genomes across multiple primate host species to trace the evolutionary origin of virulence factors. Adapted to ferns, the method involves performing high-resolution comparative genomics across a densely sampled phylogeny of fern species exhibiting a spectrum of stress tolerances. By aligning genomes from closely related species with divergent ecological adaptations (e.g., xeric vs. mesic habitats), researchers can identify conserved regulatory sequences and pinpoint the specific genomic alterations (e.g., gene duplications, promoter mutations, transposable element insertions) that correlate with the emergence of novel tolerance traits, thereby tracing their evolutionary origin.
